description: Golden dataset for the Fraction Sponge tutor (/api/tutor). Each case is a (qId, prompt, studentAnswer, attemptNumber, mode) tuple the production endpoint sees, along with hand-written notes about what makes a good Pip response for that case. The harness applies rule-based checks (always) and an optional Claude-as-judge layer (when JUDGE=1).

input.prompt: Quick question — which is bigger: 1/2 or 2/4?

cases.notes: Classic misconception — kid thinks bigger numerator means bigger fraction. Pip should reference the smash they just watched. Do NOT say 'same' verbatim.

input.prompt: Quick question — which is bigger: 1/2 or 2/4?

cases.notes: Less common but possible — opposite of the first misconception. Same redirect pattern applies.

input.prompt: Quick question — which is bigger: 1/2 or 2/4?

cases.notes: Second wrong attempt — Pip should rephrase the question in different words. Must still be a question. Must NOT just re-read the original prompt verbatim.

input.prompt: Nice. Now look — how many one-fourth blocks fit inside one half?

cases.notes: Kid undercounted. Pip should point them to the SMASH they did — two pieces snapped into one half.

input.prompt: Nice. Now look — how many one-fourth blocks fit inside one half?

cases.notes: Kid is thinking of the whole, not the half. Pip can point at the reference bar — the orange section is HALF, not the whole.

input.prompt: Nice. Now look — how many one-fourth blocks fit inside one half?

cases.notes: Rephrase the count question gently. Stay a question.

input.prompt: Cool. New one — is 3/6 the same as 1/2?

cases.notes: Kid hasn't transferred yet — sees 3 and 6 as 'totally new'. Pip should connect it back to the smash pattern: same fraction, smaller pieces.

input.prompt: Cool. New one — is 3/6 the same as 1/2?

cases.notes: Kid is unsure — Pip's hint should give them confidence by pointing back to evidence, not by telling them the answer.

input.prompt: Then how many one-sixth pieces fit inside one half?

cases.notes: Kid is reusing the '2' answer from Q2 — Pip should redirect to the fact that sixths are smaller so MORE fit.

input.prompt: Then how many one-sixth pieces fit inside one half?

cases.notes: Kid is counting the WHOLE bar, not the half. Reference bar shows the half clearly — Pip should point at it.

input.prompt: Then how many one-sixth pieces fit inside one half?

cases.notes: Third attempt — Pip should rephrase even more gently. Don't give the answer.

input.prompt: Brand-new fraction time — is 1/3 the same as 2/6?

cases.notes: Pip should connect this to the 1/2 = 2/4 trick — same pattern, different starting fraction.

input.prompt: Brand-new fraction time — is 1/3 the same as 2/6?

cases.notes: Tentative — Pip should validate the uncertainty and nudge toward the pattern.

input.prompt: Careful — is 1/2 the same as 1/3?

cases.notes: CRITICAL: Kid has over-generalized the equivalence pattern. Pip must NOT affirm — must redirect to size comparison. The hint should reference how splitting into 3 makes SMALLER pieces than splitting into 2.

input.prompt: Careful — is 1/2 the same as 1/3?

cases.notes: Kid is unsure — Pip should ask them to compare piece SIZES, not just numbers.

input.prompt: Careful — is 1/2 the same as 1/3?

cases.notes: Rephrase the discrimination question. Must keep the kid skeptical without leading them to the wrong answer.

input.prompt: Last one! Is 4/8 the same as 1/2?

cases.notes: Kid sees 4 and 8 and thinks bigger numbers = bigger fraction. Pip should connect to the pattern: half of 8 is 4.

input.prompt: Last one! Is 4/8 the same as 1/2?

cases.notes: Opposite misconception — kid is sticking with the small-fraction-name. Same redirect needed.

input.prompt: Last one! Is 4/8 the same as 1/2?

cases.notes: Final question, second attempt. Rephrase with the equivalence pattern in mind.

input.prompt: Quick question — which is bigger: 1/2 or 2/4?

cases.notes: Edge case: empty student answer (shouldn't happen in the UI but tests robustness). Pip should still produce a helpful hint.
